title: Ginger biscuits
subtitle: Rolled, cut, and spiced with ground ginger and golden syrup
excerpt: Twenty-four ginger biscuits: a melted-method dough of butter, golden syrup, and brown sugar, spiced with ground ginger and a little cinnamon, rolled to 4 mm and cut into rounds. They come out of the oven still soft and firm up completely as they cool.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the ginger biscuit section documents a dough made with melted butter, treacle or syrup, and ground ginger, rolled and cut into rounds. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's gingerbread biscuit section documents the melted-method technique as the standard approach for spiced rolled biscuits in British domestic cookery: fat, sugar, and syrup are melted together before the dry ingredients are incorporated. The recipe here follows the public-domain method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked, cooled biscuits in a bag, layered with baking paper. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Bake in separate batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough keeps refrigerated for 3 days, or freeze and defrost before rolling.

recipeTools.notes: A 6 cm round cutter, or a gingerbread cutter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process by which biscuits firm up as they cool on the rack after coming out of the oven. Biscuits that look and feel underdone when they come out , still pale and slightly soft in the centre, will usually be correct once completely cooled. Over-baking to achieve the final texture in the oven produces hard, brittle biscuits. The sign a ginger biscuit is ready: golden at the edges, set enough to hold its shape when lifted, even though it is still soft.

content.text: Ginger biscuits use a melted-method dough: butter, golden syrup, and sugar are melted together before the flour and spice go in. The result is a pliable, fudgy dough that chills to a rollable consistency. The biscuits come out of the oven still soft at the centre — this is correct.
does the rest as they cool on the rack.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{light-muscovado-sugar}} together in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes — it should be warm, not hot. Pour the melted mixture and the {{eggs}} beaten egg into the dry ingredients and stir with a spoon until a soft dough forms. It will be sticky. Press into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes until firm.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan and line two baking trays with baking paper. On a lightly floured surface, roll the chilled dough to 4 mm thick — not thinner, or the biscuits will be fragile. Cut with a 6 cm round cutter and lay on the prepared trays with 2 cm between each. Gather and re-roll the scraps to cut more biscuits.

Bake for 10–12 minutes, until the edges are just set and the surface has cracked slightly — the centres will still look soft. This is correct: they will firm to a crisp snap as they cool. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. Once cooled completely, they should snap cleanly. Store in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks.
